Optical sensors do not inherently measure the electrical or 3D properties of the finger and are more vulnerable to 2D spoof attacks — a high-resolution printed fingerprint image or a thin silicone overlay can deceive some optical sensor implementations.

**Ultrasonic sensors** use high-frequency sound waves (typically in the 5–10 MHz range) to capture a 3D image of the fingerprint. The sensor emits an ultrasonic pulse that penetrates the skin surface. The acoustic impedance difference between ridges, valleys, and sub-surface tissue creates reflected signals that the sensor reconstructs into a 3D depth map of the fingerprint.

Qualcomm's **3D Sonic** sensor family (3D Sonic Gen 1 and Gen 2) is the dominant ultrasonic implementation, deployed in Samsung Galaxy S flagship devices (S10 and later), and select other Android flagships. Ultrasonic sensors offer superior performance with wet, oily, or dirty fingers because sound waves penetrate surface contaminants that would obscure optical or capacitive readings. The 3D depth information also provides intrinsic anti-spoofing capability — the sensor can detect the volumetric ridge structure rather than just the surface pattern.

| Dimension | Capacitive | Optical (Under-Display) | Ultrasonic |
|:----------|:-----------|:-----------------------|:-----------|
| **Physical principle** | Measures capacitance difference between ridges and valleys | Captures 2D reflected light image | Captures 3D depth map via ultrasonic pulse reflection |
| **Placement** | Side-mounted, rear-mounted, home button | Under OLED display | Under OLED display |
| **FAR** | ~1/50,000 (0.002%) | ~1/10,000–1/2,000 (0.01–0.05%) | ~1/100,000 (0.001% — Qualcomm published) |
| **FRR** | ~2–3% | ~3–5% (depends on generation) | ~2–3% |
| **Wet/dirty finger performance** | Moderate — moisture affects capacitance | Poor — water/oil distorts reflected image | Good — ultrasonic penetrates surface contaminants |
| **2D spoof resistance** | Moderate — measures electrical properties; printed photos fail | Low — most vulnerable to printed fingerprints and silicone overlays | High — 3D depth map defeats flat reproductions |
| **Speed** | Fast (~200–300ms) | Moderate (~300–500ms for under-display) | Fast (~200–300ms for Gen 2) |

##### 12.1.2 Minutiae-Based Matching

Fingerprint matching algorithms do not compare raw fingerprint images. Instead, they extract **minutiae** — distinctive local features of the ridge pattern — and compare the spatial relationships between these features. The minutiae extraction and matching approach is the dominant algorithm family, standardised in ISO/IEC 19794-2 (Fingerprint Minutiae Data).

**Minutiae types:**

| Minutiae Type | Description | Frequency |
|:-------------|:-----------|:----------|
| **Ridge ending** | A ridge that terminates abruptly | ~50% of minutiae |
| **Ridge bifurcation** | A ridge that splits into two ridges at a Y-junction | ~50% of minutiae |
| **Short ridge (dot)** | A ridge significantly shorter than the average ridge length | Rare — some algorithms classify these; others ignore them |
| **Island** | A short ridge enclosed by two bifurcations | Rare |
| **Spur** | A bifurcation with a short branch | Rare |
| **Crossover** | Two ridges crossing each other | Rare |

In practice, most matching algorithms use only ridge endings and bifurcations — the two most common and most reliably extractable minutiae types. Each minutia is characterised by three parameters: **position** (x, y coordinates relative to a reference point or the image centre), **angle** (the orientation of the ridge at the minutia point, measured in degrees or radians), and **type** (ending or bifurcation).

**Template format (ISO/IEC 19794-2):**

A fingerprint minutiae template — the data structure stored for matching — contains the set of extracted minutiae points. Each minutia record encodes:
- Position: x, y coordinates (2 bytes each)
- Angle: ridge direction at the minutia (1 byte, quantised to 256 levels = ~1.4° resolution)
- Type: ridge ending or bifurcation (1 bit, packed with quality metadata)
- Quality: a per-minutia confidence score

A typical fingerprint yields 20–80 minutiae points. The template stores the complete set, producing a **compact representation** of typically **500–1,000 bytes** per fingerprint — orders of magnitude smaller than the original image (typically 150–300 KB at 500 DPI). This compact size enables efficient storage and comparison, and critically, the template cannot be reverse-engineered to reconstruct the original fingerprint image — the transformation from image to minutiae is lossy and one-directional.

##### 12.1.3 Performance Metrics

Biometric system performance is characterised by three fundamental metrics, defined in ISO/IEC 19795-1 (Biometric Testing and Reporting):

- **False Acceptance Rate (FAR)** — also called **False Match Rate (FMR)**: the probability that the system incorrectly accepts an impostor as a genuine user. FAR measures the security of the system — a lower FAR means fewer unauthorised accesses
- **False Rejection Rate (FRR)** — also called **False Non-Match Rate (FNMR)**: the probability that the system incorrectly rejects a genuine user. FRR measures the usability of the system — a lower FRR means fewer legitimate users locked out
- **Equal Error Rate (EER)**: the operating point where FAR = FRR. EER provides a single-number summary of system accuracy — a lower EER indicates a more accurate system. However, real deployments do not operate at the EER point; they tune the decision threshold to favour either security (lower FAR, higher FRR) or usability (lower FRR, higher FAR)

These metrics are **inversely related** — tightening the matching threshold to reduce FAR necessarily increases FRR, and vice versa. The threshold is a configurable parameter that the system operator selects based on the risk profile of the application.

##### 12.1.4 Standard Compliance

**ISO/IEC 19794-2 — Fingerprint Minutiae Data:**

ISO/IEC 19794-2 defines the standardised data format for fingerprint minutiae templates, enabling interoperability between different vendors' fingerprint systems. The standard specifies the record format, coordinate systems, minutiae encoding, and quality metadata. Two encoding formats are defined:

- **Record format** — a binary format for compact storage and transmission
- **XML format** — for integration with identity management systems and biometric databases

In consumer mobile device contexts, vendor-proprietary template formats are used rather than ISO/IEC 19794-2 — Apple's Secure Enclave stores fingerprint templates in a proprietary format that never leaves the SE, and Android's TEE/StrongBox implementations use vendor-specific template encodings. ISO/IEC 19794-2 is primarily relevant for government identity programmes (e-passports, national ID cards), law enforcement (AFIS — Automated Fingerprint Identification Systems), and cross-organisational biometric interoperability.

#### 12.2 Facial Recognition (2D, 3D/Structured Light, Infrared)

Facial recognition encompasses a broad range of technologies — from basic 2D camera-based matching (vulnerable to photographs) to advanced 3D structured light systems (resistant to all known 2D and most 3D attacks). The security characteristics vary dramatically across implementations, and the distinction between "face unlock" on a budget Android phone and Apple's Face ID is a difference in kind, not merely degree.

##### 12.2.1 2D Camera-Based Facial Recognition

The simplest form of facial recognition uses the standard front-facing RGB camera to capture a 2D image of the user's face. The image is compared against a stored reference using neural network-based feature extraction — the system encodes the facial geometry (distance between eyes, nose shape, jawline contour) into a mathematical embedding vector and compares it against the enrolled template using cosine similarity or Euclidean distance.

**Android 2D Face Unlock:** Most Android devices that offer "face unlock" use the standard front-facing camera without depth sensing. The Android Compatibility Definition Document (CDD) classifies these implementations as **Class 1 (Convenience)** or at best **Class 2 (Weak)** biometrics — they **cannot unlock hardware-backed cryptographic keys**. The biometric classification is:

| Class | Strength | SAR Requirement | FAR Requirement | Can Unlock Hardware Keys |
|:------|:---------|:----------------|:----------------|:------------------------|
| Class 1 (Convenience) | Minimal | No PAD testing required | FAR < 1% (1/100) | ❌ No |
| Class 2 (Weak) | Low | SAR < 20% (Level A PAI) | FAR < 0.5% (1/200) | ❌ No |
| Class 3 (Strong) | High | SAR < 7% (Level A/B PAI) | FAR < 0.002% (1/50,000) | ✅ Yes |

2D face unlock on standard Android devices fails the Class 3 SAR requirements because a simple printed photograph or a video replay on a screen can reliably fool the system. The system provides convenience (quick device unlock for low-risk operations) but does **not** provide security suitable for cryptographic key protection or financial transactions.

**Vulnerability:** 2D camera-based facial recognition is vulnerable to:
- Printed photographs (held in front of the camera)
- Screen replays (displaying a photo or video on another device)
- 2D masks (paper or fabric with the owner's face printed on them)

##### 12.2.2 3D Structured Light (Apple Face ID)

Apple's Face ID — introduced with iPhone X (2017) and present on all Face ID-equipped iPhones, iPads, and the Vision Pro — uses the **TrueDepth camera system**, a dedicated sensor array that projects approximately **30,000 invisible near-infrared (NIR) dots** onto the user's face in a known pattern. A NIR camera captures the deformation of the dot pattern as it conforms to the facial surface, enabling the system to construct a precise **3D depth map** of the face.

**TrueDepth camera components:**

| Component | Function |
|:----------|:---------|
| **Flood illuminator** | NIR LED that illuminates the face — enables the system to detect a face even in complete darkness |
| **Dot projector** | Projects ~30,000 NIR dots in a known pattern onto the face surface |
| **NIR camera** | Captures the deformed dot pattern and the flood-illuminated NIR image |
| **Neural Engine** | Dedicated hardware neural network accelerator on the Apple A-series/M-series SoC — performs the 3D face matching computation |

**Security characteristics:**

- **FAR:** 1 in 1,000,000 (Apple's published specification) — compared to 1 in 50,000 for Touch ID. Face ID is twenty times more selective than fingerprint recognition
- **3D depth requirement:** The system requires a genuine 3D facial structure. Flat photographs, screen replays, and 2D masks fail because they do not produce the correct depth pattern when illuminated with the structured light projector
- **Attention awareness:** Face ID requires the user's eyes to be **open and looking at the device** — the system detects gaze direction using the NIR image. This prevents unlock while the user is sleeping, unconscious, or looking away, and defeats attacks where an attacker holds the phone up to an unaware victim's face. Attention awareness can be disabled in Accessibility settings for users who cannot make eye contact (certain physical disabilities)
- **Adaptive learning:** The Neural Engine continuously updates the enrolled face template to accommodate gradual changes — growing a beard, changing hairstyles, wearing glasses, ageing. The system maintains a running collection of recent successful match representations and uses them to supplement the original enrollment template

**Template storage:** The mathematical representation of the face — not an image — is stored in the **Secure Enclave** (§11.2.1). The template cannot be extracted from the SE by any mechanism. Face ID samples are processed in the SE's encrypted memory and are never exposed to the application processor or transmitted to Apple's servers.

**Anti-spoofing testing:** Apple has tested Face ID against custom-manufactured 3D masks created by professional special-effects studios and mask-making companies. The system resists these attacks because the depth map matching is sensitive to sub-millimetre surface geometry differences, material reflectance properties under NIR illumination, and the presence of living tissue characteristics (skin reflectance spectrum in NIR).

##### 12.2.3 Infrared Camera (Windows Hello)

Windows Hello facial recognition uses a **near-infrared (NIR) camera** paired with an IR illuminator to capture a face image that is robust to ambient lighting conditions. Unlike Apple's structured light approach (which projects a dot pattern and measures deformation), Windows Hello's IR approach captures a standard 2D image — but in the near-infrared spectrum rather than visible light.

**Advantages of NIR imaging:**
- **Lighting independence:** NIR illumination provides consistent lighting regardless of ambient conditions — the system works in complete darkness, bright sunlight, and mixed lighting
- **Photograph resistance:** Printed photographs and screen displays do not reflect NIR light in the same pattern as live skin — the ink/toner on a photograph absorbs NIR differently than human skin tissue, and LCD/OLED screens emit visible light but not NIR. This provides inherent resistance to simple photograph attacks without requiring a 3D depth sensor
- **Glasses transparency:** Standard eyeglasses are transparent to NIR, allowing the system to capture detailed eye and orbital geometry even when the user is wearing glasses

**Security classification:** Windows Hello facial recognition is classified as a **strong biometric** on devices with **Windows Hello Enhanced Sign-in Security (ESS)** — these devices have specialised cameras with hardware-isolated image processing paths where biometric data flows directly to the VBS (Virtualisation Based Security) enclave without passing through the main OS kernel. On non-ESS devices, the security classification depends on the camera hardware and driver implementation.

**Key storage:** Windows Hello facial recognition unlocks keys stored in the **TPM** (§11.2.3). The TPM-resident key is used for authentication with Microsoft Entra ID, on-premises Active Directory, or FIDO2-based services.

#### 12.3 Iris Scanning

Iris recognition exploits the complex, random texture of the iris — the coloured muscular ring surrounding the pupil. The iris stroma contains a unique pattern of crypts, furrows, freckles, and collarette features that forms during foetal development and remains stable throughout life (barring injury or disease). Iris patterns are statistically independent even between the two eyes of the same individual, and between identical twins.

##### 12.3.1 Capture and Encoding (Daugman's Algorithm)

The dominant iris recognition algorithm was developed by John Daugman (University of Cambridge, 1993) and remains the basis for virtually all commercial iris recognition systems:

1. **Image capture:** A near-infrared (NIR) camera illuminates the eye with NIR light (700–900nm wavelength). NIR is used because melanin in the iris is transparent to NIR, allowing the iris texture to be captured regardless of eye colour — dark brown irises that appear uniform in visible light reveal complex texture patterns under NIR illumination

2. **Segmentation:** The algorithm localises the iris boundaries — the inner boundary (pupil/iris) and the outer boundary (iris/sclera) — using integrodifferential operators. Eyelids and eyelashes that occlude the iris are detected and masked

3. **Normalisation:** The annular iris region is unwrapped into a rectangular strip using Daugman's rubber-sheet model — a polar-to-Cartesian transformation that maps the iris region from its natural annular shape to a fixed-size rectangular representation, compensating for pupil dilation and capture distance variations

4. **Feature extraction:** 2D Gabor wavelets are applied to the normalised iris image. The phase response of the Gabor filter at each spatial location is quantised to 2 bits (representing four quadrants of the complex plane). The complete set of phase bits forms the **IrisCode** — a **2,048-bit (256-byte) binary template**. An equal-sized masking code indicates which bits correspond to valid (unoccluded) iris regions

5. **Matching:** Comparison between two IrisCodes uses the **Hamming distance** — the fraction of bits that differ between the two codes (considering only unmasked bits). A Hamming distance below a decision threshold (typically 0.32–0.35) indicates a match; the theoretical random-match probability between two unrelated IrisCodes is approximately **1 in 10¹²** — making iris recognition the most discriminating biometric modality

##### 12.3.2 Performance and Deployment

Iris recognition achieves theoretical false match rates that far exceed any other biometric modality. Daugman's published research, based on over 200 billion cross-comparisons, demonstrated a false match rate of less than **1 in 200 billion** at a Hamming distance threshold of 0.26 — a level of discriminative power unmatched by fingerprint, face, or any other biometric.

**Major deployments:**

- **India's Aadhaar programme** — the world's largest biometric identity system, with over 1.4 billion enrolled individuals. Aadhaar uses dual-iris capture (both eyes) for de-duplication and verification. The dual-iris approach provides a match probability of approximately 1 in 10²⁴ (quadrupling the bit-independence), enabling reliable de-duplication across a population exceeding one billion
- **UAE immigration** — iris recognition at border control gates for automated entry/exit processing
- **Samsung Galaxy S8/S9** — Samsung implemented iris scanning in its flagship smartphones (2017–2018). The feature was **discontinued after the Galaxy S9/Note 9** generation due to poor UX (required specific eye positioning and holding the phone at a precise distance), slow capture compared to fingerprint, failure in bright sunlight (ambient NIR saturated the sensor), and the competitive superiority of under-display fingerprint sensors and 3D facial recognition for mobile use

##### 12.3.3 Standard Compliance

**ISO/IEC 19794-6 — Iris Image Data:** Defines the standardised data format for iris image records, including cropped iris images and rectilinear (unwrapped) iris images. Used in e-passports (ICAO Doc 9303 — iris is one of the three ICAO-standardised biometric modalities alongside face and fingerprint), border control systems, and government identity programmes.

#### 12.4 Biometric Modality Comparison

The following table consolidates the key characteristics of the three physiological biometric modalities deployed in consumer authentication:

| Dimension | Fingerprint (Capacitive) | Face (3D Structured Light) | Iris |
|:----------|:------------------------|:--------------------------|:-----|
| **Sensor technology** | Capacitive plate array | NIR dot projector + camera | NIR camera + illuminator |
| **Template size** | 500–1,000 bytes (minutiae) | ~30 KB (neural embedding) | 256 bytes (IrisCode) |
| **FAR (published)** | ~1/50,000 (capacitive) | 1/1,000,000 (Apple Face ID) | ~1/10¹² (theoretical) |
| **FRR (typical)** | 2–3% | <1% (Face ID) | 0.5–2% (controlled conditions) |
| **EER** | 0.1–0.5% | <0.1% (Face ID) | <0.01% (lab conditions) |
| **Phishing resistance** | ✅ (biometric is local; cannot be replayed remotely) | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Photo spoof resistance** | ✅ (capacitive) / ❌ (optical) | ✅ (3D) / ❌ (2D camera) | ✅ (NIR imaging) |
| **3D mask spoof resistance** | N/A | ✅ (Face ID tested against professional masks) | N/A |
| **Works in darkness** | ✅ (capacitive — no light needed) | ✅ (NIR illuminator) | ✅ (NIR illuminator) |
| **Wet/dirty conditions** | Moderate (capacitive) to good (ultrasonic) | ✅ (non-contact) | ✅ (non-contact) |
| **User effort** | Low — place finger on sensor | Very low — glance at device | High — precise eye alignment |
| **Speed** | ~200–300ms | ~300ms (Face ID) | ~500ms–1s |
| **Mobile platform support** | ✅ iOS (Touch ID) + ✅ Android | ✅ iOS (Face ID) + ⚠️ Android (mostly Class 1/2) | ❌ Discontinued on mobile |
| **ISO template standard** | ISO/IEC 19794-2 | No interoperable standard | ISO/IEC 19794-6 |

#### 12.5 Multi-Modal Biometrics: Independent vs. Combined Binding

Modern devices frequently support multiple biometric modalities — a smartphone may have both a fingerprint sensor and facial recognition, or a laptop may support both fingerprint and Windows Hello face. The architectural question is how multiple modalities relate to a single cryptographic key.

##### 12.5.1 Independent Binding (OR Logic)

In independent binding, each biometric modality independently unlocks the same key — the user can authenticate with fingerprint **OR** face. The key has a single access policy that accepts any registered modality that meets the required biometric strength class. This is the **dominant model** on both iOS and Android.

**On iOS:** When a key is protected with `kSecAccessControlBiometryAny` or `kSecAccessControlBiometryCurrentSet`, either Face ID or Touch ID (on devices that support both — currently limited to certain iPad models) can unlock the key. The Secure Enclave maintains separate templates for each modality but evaluates them against a unified access policy.

**On Android:** When a key specifies `AUTH_BIOMETRIC_STRONG`, any Class 3 biometric — fingerprint, face (if Class 3 certified), iris — can unlock the key. The `BiometricPrompt` API presents whichever modality is available and meets the strength requirement.

**Implication:** Independent binding maximises usability — the user always has a fallback modality if one fails (e.g., wet fingers → use face). The security posture is determined by the **weakest** accepted modality — if face recognition is Class 2 but fingerprint is Class 3, and the key accepts either, the effective security is Class 2.

##### 12.5.2 Combined Binding (AND Logic)

Combined binding requires **multiple biometric modalities to be presented** — fingerprint **AND** face — before a key is unlocked. This significantly increases security (an attacker must defeat both modalities simultaneously) but is rarely implemented in consumer products due to the UX friction of requiring two separate biometric presentations.

**Deployments:** Combined multi-modal biometrics are used in:
- High-security government and military access control (e.g., SCIF — Sensitive Compartmented Information Facility — entry may require iris + fingerprint)
- Nuclear facility access (NRC regulations may require two-factor biometric verification)
- Some banking vault access systems

Neither iOS nor Android provides a public API for combined biometric binding — the platform APIs treat all biometric modalities as interchangeable under a single access policy.

##### 12.5.3 Score-Level Fusion

Score-level fusion combines match scores from multiple modalities to improve overall accuracy without requiring explicit AND logic. A decision engine receives the match score from each modality (e.g., fingerprint match score = 0.87, face match score = 0.92) and applies a fusion rule to produce a combined score:

- **Sum rule:** `combined = w₁ × score_fingerprint + w₂ × score_face` (weighted sum — most common in research)
- **Product rule:** `combined = score_fingerprint × score_face`
- **Max rule:** `combined = max(score_fingerprint, score_face)`

NIST multi-modal biometric fusion studies demonstrate that score-level fusion can reduce FRR by 30–50% with minimal FAR impact — the complementary error patterns of different modalities (fingerprint fails with wet fingers; face fails with masks/sunglasses) cancel out when fused.

Score-level fusion is primarily deployed in enterprise access control systems and government biometric verification programmes — not in consumer mobile platforms, which use the simpler independent binding model.

##### 12.5.4 Template-Level Independence

Even in independent binding, each modality's template is stored separately — the fingerprint template and face template are **distinct objects** in the Secure Enclave or TEE/StrongBox. Enrolling a new fingerprint does not modify or invalidate the face template, and vice versa. The independence applies at the template layer; the key access policy operates at a higher abstraction level, accepting authentication from any enrolled modality that satisfies the policy constraints.

#### 12.6 Modality-Agnostic Platforms: When the OS Hides Which Modality Was Used

Both iOS and Android present a **modality-agnostic authentication API** to applications. The application requests "authenticate the user" and the operating system determines which biometric modality to invoke based on device capabilities, user enrollment state, and sensor availability. The application receives a binary success/failure result (and, in the case of key-bound authentication, a cryptographic operation result) — it does **not** receive information about which specific biometric modality performed the authentication.

##### 12.6.1 iOS: LocalAuthentication Framework

The iOS `LAContext` API provides two authentication policies:

```swift
let context = LAContext()

// Policy 1: Biometric only — no passcode fallback
context.evaluatePolicy(
    .deviceOwnerAuthenticationWithBiometrics,
    localizedReason: "Authenticate to sign the transaction"
) { success, error in
    // success = true if Face ID/Touch ID succeeded
    // The API does NOT reveal whether Face ID or Touch ID was used
}

// Policy 2: Biometric with device passcode fallback
context.evaluatePolicy(
    .deviceOwnerAuthentication,
    localizedReason: "Authenticate to access your account"
) { success, error in
    // Falls back to device passcode if biometric fails or is unavailable
}
```

The application can query `context.biometryType` to determine which biometric modality is **available** on the device (`LABiometryType.faceID`, `.touchID`, or `.none`) — this is useful for displaying the correct icon and instructional text in the UI. However, this only reveals the device's hardware capability, not which modality was actually used for a specific authentication event. On devices with a single biometric modality (all current iPhones have Face ID; only older iPhones and some iPads have Touch ID), this distinction is academic — but it becomes relevant on iPad models that support both.

##### 12.6.2 Android: BiometricPrompt

Android's `BiometricPrompt` API (introduced in Android 9, Pie) provides a system-managed authentication dialog that is consistent across all Android devices:

```kotlin
val promptInfo = BiometricPrompt.PromptInfo.Builder()
    .setTitle("Authenticate")
    .setSubtitle("Use your biometric to sign the transaction")
    .setAllowedAuthenticators(
        BiometricManager.Authenticators.BIOMETRIC_STRONG
    )
    .setNegativeButtonText("Cancel")
    .build()

biometricPrompt.authenticate(promptInfo, cryptoObject)
```

The application specifies the **minimum strength class** (`BIOMETRIC_STRONG`, `BIOMETRIC_WEAK`, or `DEVICE_CREDENTIAL` as a fallback) but not the specific modality. The OS selects the available modality that meets the specified strength requirement and presents the appropriate system-managed UI — fingerprint animation, face scanning indicator, or iris scanning guide as applicable.

**Key property:** The `CryptoObject` parameter binds the biometric authentication to a specific cryptographic operation — the application presents a `Cipher`, `Signature`, or `Mac` object initialised with a hardware-backed key. A successful biometric authentication unlocks the key within the TEE/StrongBox, and the cryptographic operation completes within the hardware security boundary. Without the `CryptoObject`, the biometric authentication provides only a boolean result with no cryptographic binding — the app must trust the OS-level signal, which is weaker than a hardware-attested cryptographic proof.

##### 12.6.3 Why Modality-Agnostic Design

The modality-agnostic approach serves three design goals:

1. **App development simplicity:** Application developers write a single authentication codepath regardless of the device's biometric hardware. An app built for iPhone 8 (Touch ID) works identically on iPhone 15 (Face ID) without code changes
2. **Graceful fallback:** If the primary modality fails (e.g., fingerprint fails with wet fingers), the system can automatically offer an alternative (e.g., face) without the application needing to manage modality-specific retry logic
3. **Future-proofing:** New biometric modalities (e.g., palm vein, ear canal acoustic) can be added to the platform without requiring application updates — the OS classifies the new modality into the existing strength class hierarchy and exposes it through the same API

**Security implication:** The application trusts the OS to enforce the correct biometric strength class — it cannot independently verify which modality was used or how strong the match was. This is a deliberate architectural choice: the platform vendor (Apple, Google) certifies biometric sensor quality at the hardware level and enforces classification at the HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer) level, removing the verification burden from application developers.

#### 12.7 Modality-Specific Binding: Per-Biometric Key Protection

Some high-security use cases contemplate binding a cryptographic key to a **specific biometric modality** — not merely "any biometric" but "specifically fingerprint" or "specifically face." The motivation is regulatory and risk-management driven: if a user enrolled with their fingerprint for a high-value transaction signing key, the institution may require that the same modality (fingerprint) be used for every subsequent authentication — not an alternative modality that may have different security properties.

##### 12.7.1 Current Platform Capabilities

Neither iOS nor Android provides a public API to bind a key exclusively to a specific biometric modality:

| Platform | Binding Capability | Mechanism |
|:---------|:------------------|:----------|
| **iOS** | Template-set binding | `kSecAccessControlBiometryCurrentSet` — key is invalidated if the biometric enrollment set changes (e.g., new fingerprint added, Face ID re-enrolled). Any enrolled biometric can still unlock the key; the constraint is that the **set** must remain unchanged |
| **iOS** | Any-biometric binding | `kSecAccessControlBiometryAny` — key survives biometric enrollment changes. Less strict — a new biometric identity added after key creation can unlock the key |
| **Android** | Enrollment-set binding | `setInvalidatedByBiometricEnrollment(true)` — key is invalidated if any new biometric is enrolled. Equivalent to iOS's `BiometryCurrentSet` — the key is bound to the current enrollment set, not to a specific modality |
| **Android** | Strength-class binding | `setUserAuthenticationParameters(0, AUTH_BIOMETRIC_STRONG)` — only Class 3 biometrics can unlock the key. This ensures minimum quality but does not restrict to a specific modality |

**The gap:** No standard platform API allows an application to specify "this key can only be unlocked by fingerprint, not by face" or "this key requires iris, not fingerprint." The platform treats all enrolled biometrics of the required strength class as interchangeable.

##### 12.7.2 Regulatory Interpretation (PSD2)

PSD2 RTS (Delegated Regulation (EU) 2018/389) Article 4 requires that the authentication elements used for Strong Customer Authentication (SCA) satisfy three categories: knowledge, possession, and inherence. Article 8 requires that the personalised security credentials used during dynamic linking (transaction signing) be specific to the amount and payee.

The regulatory question is: if a user enrolled a fingerprint as their inherence factor during SCA enrollment but subsequently authenticates with facial recognition (because the platform permits either modality), has the inherence element changed?

**Industry consensus:** Most European regulators (EBA, DNB, BaFin, AFM) and the EBA's published opinions treat the device's biometric system as a **single inherence factor**, regardless of which specific modality is used. The rationale is that the biometric is bound to the device's hardware security boundary (SE/TEE) — the inherence factor is "biometric authentication on this specific device," not "this specific fingerprint." The key's access policy enforces hardware-backed biometric authentication; the specific modality is an implementation detail within the hardware security boundary.

**Exception:** Some national competent authorities (e.g., BaFin in certain interpretive communications) have expressed a preference for modality consistency — that the inherence factor used during transaction signing should be the same modality registered during SCA enrollment. In practice, this guidance has not been enforced as a blocking requirement because the platform APIs do not support modality-specific binding.

#### 12.8 Platform Constraints: iOS LocalAuthentication vs. Android BiometricPrompt

This section provides a deep feature comparison of the two dominant platform biometric APIs — the architectures that mediate between the application layer and the biometric hardware.

| Dimension | iOS (LocalAuthentication + Security framework) | Android (BiometricPrompt + Keystore) |
|:----------|:----------------------------------------------|:-------------------------------------|
| **Primary authentication API** | `LAContext.evaluatePolicy()` for boolean auth; `SecKeyCreateRandomKey` with `.biometryCurrentSet` for key-bound auth | `BiometricPrompt.authenticate()` with `CryptoObject` for key-bound auth |
| **Modality selection** | System-controlled — app cannot force a specific modality | System-controlled — app cannot force a specific modality |
| **Strength classification** | Not exposed — all biometrics on Apple devices are implicitly Class 3 equivalent (Face ID and Touch ID both meet Apple's internal quality bar) | Explicitly classified: Class 1 (Convenience), Class 2 (Weak), Class 3 (Strong). Only Class 3 unlocks hardware-backed keys |
| **Fallback to device credential** | Configurable: `.deviceOwnerAuthentication` allows passcode fallback; `.deviceOwnerAuthenticationWithBiometrics` is biometric-only | Configurable: `BIOMETRIC_STRONG \| DEVICE_CREDENTIAL` allows PIN/pattern fallback |
| **Hardware key binding** | Keys with `kSecAttrTokenIDSecureEnclave` reside in the Secure Enclave; biometric enforced by SE | Keys with `setUserAuthenticationRequired(true)` + `AUTH_BIOMETRIC_STRONG` reside in TEE/StrongBox; biometric enforced by KeyMint HAL |
| **Key invalidation on enrollment change** | `kSecAccessControlBiometryCurrentSet` → invalidated on any biometric change | `setInvalidatedByBiometricEnrollment(true)` → invalidated on new enrollment |
| **Biometric reuse timeout** | `LAContext.touchIDAuthenticationAllowableReuseDuration` — configurable window during which a previous biometric auth is reused without re-prompting (max 5 minutes for LAPolicyDeviceOwnerAuthenticationWithBiometrics) | `setUserAuthenticationParameters(timeout, type)` — `timeout = 0` requires per-use auth; `timeout > 0` allows reuse within window |
| **Negative result handling** | LAError codes: `.biometryNotAvailable`, `.biometryNotEnrolled`, `.biometryLockout`, `.authenticationFailed` | BiometricPrompt callback: `onAuthenticationError()` with error codes: `BIOMETRIC_ERROR_HW_NOT_PRESENT`, `BIOMETRIC_ERROR_NO_BIOMETRICS`, `BIOMETRIC_ERROR_LOCKOUT` |
| **Lockout policy** | 5 failed attempts → biometric disabled; passcode required to re-enable. After excessive passcode failures → progressive time delays | Varies by OEM — typically 5 failed attempts → 30-second cooldown; after 20+ failures → biometric disabled until device credential (PIN/pattern) |
| **UI ownership** | System-managed (Face ID) or app-managed with system overlay (Touch ID) | System-managed — `BiometricPrompt` renders a standardised system dialog that the app cannot customise beyond title/subtitle text |

#### 12.9 Liveness Detection and Presentation Attack Detection (PAD)

Presentation attacks attempt to deceive a biometric sensor by presenting a non-live biometric artifact — a photograph, silicone fingerprint mold, 3D-printed mask, video replay, or deepfake-generated video. **Presentation Attack Detection (PAD)** systems distinguish live biometric presentations from artificial artifacts, and ISO/IEC 30107 provides the standardised framework for evaluating PAD effectiveness.

##### 12.9.1 ISO/IEC 30107 Framework

ISO/IEC 30107 (parts 1–4) defines the vocabulary, testing methodology, and reporting format for Presentation Attack Detection. The standard introduces key concepts:

- **Presentation Attack Instrument (PAI):** The artifact used to execute the attack — a printed photo, a silicone fingerprint mold, a 3D mask, a screen replay, a synthetic voice recording
- **PAI species:** A specific type of PAI (e.g., "printed colour photo on laser paper," "silicone fingerprint mold with gelatin coating," "3D-printed rigid mask with eye cutouts")
- **Attack Presentation Classification Error Rate (APCER):** The proportion of presentation attacks using a specific PAI species that are incorrectly classified as bona fide (genuine) presentations — lower is better (higher security)
- **Bona Fide Presentation Classification Error Rate (BPCER):** The proportion of genuine presentations that are incorrectly classified as attacks — lower is better (higher usability)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["`**Biometric Presentation**
    User interacts with sensor`"]
    
    subgraph Origin["Presentation Source"]
        C["`**Attacker (PAI)**
        Presentation Attack Instrument`"]
        D["`**Bona Fide**
        Genuine User`"]
        C ~~~ D
    end
    
    subgraph Attacks["Attack Sophistication (ISO/IEC 30107)"]
        E["`**Level A**
        Photo / Play-dough`"]
        F["`**Level B**
        Silicone / 3D Mask`"]
        G["`**Level C**
        Deepfake / Custom Mask`"]
        E ~~~ F
        F ~~~ G
    end
    
    H{"`**PAD System**
    (Active & Passive)`"}
    
    I{"`**Decision Engine**`"}
    
    subgraph Outcomes["Classification Metrics"]
        J["`**APCER**
        (Attack passed) ❌`"]
        K["`**True Reject**
        (Attack blocked) ✅`"]
        L["`**BPCER**
        (Genuine blocked) ❌`"]
        M["`**True Accept**
        (Genuine passed) ✅`"]
        J ~~~ K
        K ~~~ L
        L ~~~ M
    end

    style A text-align:left
    style C text-align:left
    style D text-align:left
    style E text-align:left
    style F text-align:left
    style G text-align:left
    style J text-align:left
    style K text-align:left
    style L text-align:left
    style M text-align:left
    
    A --> Origin
    C --> Attacks
    D --> H
    Attacks --> H
    H --> I
    
    I -->|False Accept| J
    I -->|Correct Reject| K
    I -->|False Reject| L
    I -->|Correct Accept| M
```

##### 12.9.2 PAI Levels

PAIs are categorised by attacker sophistication into three levels, guiding the testing regime:

| PAI Level | Attacker Profile | Preparation | Materials | Example PAIs |
|:----------|:----------------|:-----------|:---------|:-------------|
| **Level A** | Casual attacker — low skill, readily available materials, minimal preparation | Minutes to hours (<8 hours) | < $30 — household materials, office supplies, consumer printer | Printed colour photographs, paper masks, play-dough fingerprints, smartphone screen replay, lifted fingerprint latent dusted with powder |
| **Level B** | Informed attacker — moderate skill, specialised materials, some preparation | Hours to days | < $300 — professional printer, silicone casting materials, 3D printer (consumer grade) | 3D-printed rigid masks, silicone fingerprint molds with pore-level detail, high-resolution printed irises with contact lens overlay, pre-recorded video loops |
| **Level C** | Expert attacker — high skill, laboratory resources, extended preparation | Days to weeks | Unconstrained — professional mask-making, custom materials | Custom-manufactured silicone masks with realistic skin texture and colour, masks with eye cutouts and motorised blink mechanisms, multi-spectral replay systems, hyper-realistic deepfake video |

The Android CDD Class 3 biometric certification requires testing against **Level A and Level B PAIs** with a Spoof Acceptance Rate (SAR) below 7% for Level A and below 20% for Level B. Apple's internal Face ID testing programme includes Level C-equivalent artefacts — professionally manufactured masks — though Apple does not publish certification against external PAI level standards.

##### 12.9.3 Fingerprint Liveness Detection

| Technique | Mechanism | Effectiveness |
|:----------|:---------|:-------------|
| **Capacitive sensing** | Live skin has different dielectric properties than silicone, gelatin, or paper — capacitive sensors measure electrical impedance, not just surface topology | Effective against printed photos and most artificial materials; can be defeated by sophisticated conductive silicone replicas |
| **Pulse/blood flow detection** | Some sensors include a photoplethysmography (PPG) sensor that detects blood flow beneath the finger surface | Effective against all non-living materials; adds ~$0.50–1.00 to sensor BOM cost |
| **Sweat pore detection** | Ultrasonic sensors can resolve individual sweat pores (20–50µm) on the finger surface; artificial fingers typically lack realistic pore distribution | Effective for ultrasonic sensors; not applicable to capacitive or optical |
| **Multi-spectral imaging** | Imaging at multiple wavelengths (visible + NIR + SWIR) to identify tissue absorption characteristics unique to living skin | Highly effective; deployed in enterprise fingerprint scanners (e.g., HID Lumidigm) but not in mobile devices due to sensor cost and size |
| **Software-based (AI/ML)** | Deep learning models trained on live vs. spoof fingerprint images, applied to the sensor's raw output | Moderate — effectiveness depends on training data diversity and the sophistication of novel attack materials |

##### 12.9.4 Facial Liveness Detection

| Technique | Mechanism | Effectiveness |
|:----------|:---------|:-------------|
| **3D depth mapping** | Structured light (Face ID) or Time-of-Flight (ToF) sensors capture the 3D geometry of the face — flat replays and photographs produce no depth variation | Highly effective — defeats all 2D attacks (photos, screen replays, 2D masks). Face ID additionally tests for sub-millimetre surface geometry consistency |
| **Challenge-response (active liveness)** | Prompting the user to perform specific actions — blink, turn head left/right, smile, say a phrase | Effective against static images; **increasingly vulnerable to deepfake video** — generative AI can produce real-time facial animations responding to arbitrary prompts |
| **Texture analysis** | Analysing image texture for artifacts of screen replay (moiré patterns, pixel grid structure) or printing (halftone dot patterns, inkjet banding) | Moderate — effective against low-quality replays; sophisticated attacks using high-DPI displays or professional printing can evade texture detectors |
| **Infrared analysis** | Capturing the face under NIR illumination — live skin has specific reflectance properties in the 700–900nm range; screens (which emit visible light but not NIR) and photographs (which absorb/reflect NIR differently) produce anomalous signatures | Effective when paired with dedicated NIR illuminator (Face ID, Windows Hello) |

##### 12.9.5 The Deepfake Threat

Generative AI has fundamentally changed the threat landscape for video-based facial liveness detection. Real-time deepfake systems can generate convincing facial animations that respond to challenge-response prompts (blink, turn head, speak) with sub-second latency. This renders **active liveness checks that rely on 2D camera data increasingly unreliable**:

- **Camera injection attacks:** Malware or virtual camera software intercepts the camera feed and replaces it with a deepfake-generated video stream — the application receives a synthetic video that passes challenge-response checks without any physical presentation
- **Screen replay with deepfake:** A deepfake video of the target user is displayed on a screen in front of the device's camera — the deepfake responds to dynamic prompts in real time

**Defences that remain resistant to deepfakes:**

- **3D depth sensing (structured light, ToF):** Deepfakes cannot produce correct 3D geometry — a screen displaying a deepfake video produces a flat depth map (the distance to the screen surface), not the 3D facial contour that Face ID requires. This makes structured light and ToF-based facial recognition fundamentally resistant to deepfake attacks, regardless of the video quality
- **NIR spectral analysis:** Deepfake videos displayed on screens emit visible light but not NIR in the pattern of live skin — NIR-based liveness checks detect this discrepancy
- **Hardware integrity attestation (§13):** Device attestation can verify that the camera feed originates from the genuine hardware camera sensor, not from injected software — this counters camera injection attacks but requires end-to-end hardware-attested image paths

#### 12.10 Behavioural Biometrics (Continuous Authentication)

Behavioural biometrics authenticate users based on **how they interact with a device** rather than physiological characteristics. Unlike fingerprint, face, or iris — which provide a point-in-time positive identification — behavioural biometrics operate as a **continuous signal** that monitors interaction patterns throughout a session to detect anomalies.

##### 12.10.1 Modalities

| Modality | Measured Characteristics | Sensors |
|:---------|:------------------------|:--------|
| **Keystroke dynamics** | Key dwell time (key-down to key-up), flight time (key-up to next key-down), typing speed, rhythm patterns, error rate, shift/modifier key usage patterns | Keyboard input events (timing metadata) |
| **Mouse dynamics** | Movement speed, acceleration, curvature (path deviation from straight line), click patterns (single vs. double, pressure on pressure-sensitive trackpads), scrolling behaviour | Mouse/trackpad input events |
| **Touch screen dynamics** | Swipe speed, swipe pressure, finger contact area, gesture patterns (multi-touch), typing on soft keyboard (combines keystroke + touch dynamics), screen interaction zones | Touch screen digitiser (pressure, area, coordinates, timestamps) |
| **Gait analysis** | Walking acceleration pattern, stride frequency, step symmetry, device orientation changes during walking | Accelerometer, gyroscope, magnetometer (IMU sensors) |
| **Device interaction patterns** | App usage sequences, typical session durations, navigation patterns, time-of-day usage profiles, Wi-Fi/cell tower location consistency | OS-level analytics, network metadata |

##### 12.10.2 Use Case: Continuous Risk Scoring

Behavioural biometrics are **not used as a primary authentication factor** — their error rates are too high and too variable for positive identification. Instead, they serve as a **continuous risk signal** within an adaptive authentication framework (§20):

1. **Baseline establishment:** During the initial sessions after enrollment, the system builds a behavioural profile — the user's characteristic typing rhythm, mouse movement patterns, touch pressure, and device interaction habits
2. **Continuous monitoring:** During each subsequent session, the system compares the ongoing interaction pattern against the established baseline
3. **Anomaly detection → risk escalation:** If the behavioural pattern deviates significantly from the baseline (e.g., the typing rhythm is inconsistent, the mouse movements follow a different curvature profile, the device is being used in an unusual location), the system increases the session risk score
4. **Step-up trigger:** When the risk score exceeds a configured threshold, the system triggers **step-up authentication** (§20.5) — requiring the user to re-authenticate with a stronger factor (e.g., biometric, hardware token, OTP)

This pattern is especially valuable for detecting **account takeover** during active sessions — an attacker who has obtained valid session cookies (via info-stealer malware, AiTM phishing, or session hijacking) will authenticate successfully but will interact with the application using behavioural patterns that differ from the legitimate user's baseline.

##### 12.10.3 Accuracy Limitations

Behavioural biometrics have **significantly higher error rates** than physiological biometrics:

| Metric | Keystroke Dynamics | Mouse Dynamics | Touch Dynamics | Gait Analysis |
|:-------|:------------------|:---------------|:---------------|:--------------|
| **Typical EER** | 5–15% | 5–20% | 3–10% | 5–15% |
| **Comparison: fingerprint EER** | 0.1–0.5% | — | — | — |

These error rates are unsuitable for positive authentication — a 10% EER would mean that 1 in 10 legitimate users is rejected, or 1 in 10 impostors is accepted, depending on the threshold setting. The error rates also **vary with user state** — fatigue, injury, medication, emotional state, unfamiliar hardware (new keyboard, different mouse), and environmental factors (cold hands, wearing gloves) all affect behavioural patterns, increasing FRR.

For this reason, behavioural biometrics are used exclusively as a **probabilistic risk signal** rather than a deterministic authentication factor — the system adjusts confidence levels continuously rather than making binary accept/reject decisions.

##### 12.10.4 Privacy Considerations

Continuous behavioural monitoring raises significant privacy concerns — the system is effectively a **keylogger and interaction tracker** that records detailed information about every user interaction:

- **GDPR Article 9:** Biometric data processed for the purpose of uniquely identifying a natural person is classified as a **special category of personal data** under GDPR Article 9(1). Continuous behavioural monitoring that creates a unique user profile falls within this classification. Processing requires one of the Article 9(2) exceptions — typically **explicit consent** (Article 9(2)(a)) or **substantial public interest** (Article 9(2)(g)) — in addition to a lawful basis under Article 6
- **Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA):** Under GDPR Article 35, processing of biometric data for identification purposes is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons, triggering a mandatory DPIA requirement
- **Data minimisation:** The system must collect only the minimum behavioural data necessary for the risk assessment purpose and must not retain raw interaction data beyond the period required for profile maintenance
- **Transparency:** Users must be informed that behavioural biometric monitoring is active, what data is collected, how it is used, and how to exercise their data subject rights (access, erasure, objection)

##### 12.10.5 Commercial Implementations

| Vendor | Product | Primary Market | Approach |
|:-------|:--------|:--------------|:---------|
| **BioCatch** | BioCatch Connect | Banking and financial services | Analyses 500+ behavioural parameters during online banking sessions; detects social engineering, remote access tool (RAT) attacks, and account takeover. Used by major global banks including HSBC, NatWest, and Barclays |
| **TypingDNA** | TypingDNA Verify | Enterprise and consumer authentication | Keystroke dynamics-focused; provides typing pattern matching as a second-factor authentication signal or continuous verification layer |
| **Nuance** | Gatekeeper | Voice-based customer service | Voice biometrics — analyses vocal characteristics (pitch, cadence, accent, speaking rhythm) for caller authentication in contact centres |
| **Mastercard** | NuDetect (acquired from NuData Security) | Payment fraud detection | Combines behavioural biometrics with device intelligence for payment risk scoring — deployed at the issuer and merchant level |

---

### 13. Device Authentication and Attestation

The preceding chapters establish that cryptographic keys can be generated inside hardware security boundaries — Secure Enclave, StrongBox, TEE, TPM (§11.2) — and that biometric authentication can unlock those keys for use (§12). But a fundamental question remains: **how does a remote server know that these claims are true?** When a client asserts that a signing key was generated inside a Secure Enclave, that the device is running unmodified software, or that a biometric authentication was performed by Class 3 hardware — the server has no way to independently verify these claims without **attestation**.

Device authentication and attestation provide the cryptographic proof that bridges the gap between client-side security claims and server-side trust decisions. Attestation is a signed statement — produced by hardware or a platform service — that certifies properties of a key, a device, or a software environment. The relying party (RP) verifies the attestation by validating a certificate chain rooted in a manufacturer's or platform vendor's certificate authority, checking a server-provided nonce to prevent replay, and inspecting the attested properties to confirm they meet security requirements.

This chapter examines the three major attestation ecosystems — Android Key Attestation, Apple App Attest, and TPM 2.0 — followed by the synthesis of device-bound key lifecycle management and the device trust signal APIs that aggregate integrity verdicts for adaptive authentication decisions (§20).

#### 13.1 Android Key Attestation

Android Key Attestation allows a server to cryptographically verify that a key pair was generated inside the device's hardware security module (TEE or StrongBox) and to inspect the properties of both the key and the device at the time of key generation. The attestation mechanism is built into the Android Keystore system and is available on all devices running Android 8.0 (API level 26) and above with hardware-backed key storage.

##### 13.1.1 Attestation Certificate Chain

When an application generates a key pair in the Android Keystore with attestation enabled, the Keystore returns not just the public key but a **certificate chain** — typically three or four X.509 certificates:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0["`**Certificate 0: Key Attestation Certificate**
    **Subject**: the attested key's public key
    **Issuer**: intermediate attestation CA
    **Extension OID**: 1.3.6.1.4.1.11129.2.1.17 (KeyDescription)
    **Signed by**: intermediate CA private key (in hardware)`"]
    style C0 text-align:left

    C1["`**Certificate 1: Intermediate Attestation CA**
    **Issuer**: Google Hardware Attestation Root
    **Signed by**: root CA private key`"]
    style C1 text-align:left

    C2["`**Certificate 2: Google Hardware Attestation Root CA**
    Self-signed root certificate
    Published by Google`"]
    style C2 text-align:left

    C0 -->|Signed By| C1
    C1 -->|Signed By| C2
```

The RP validates the chain by verifying that the root certificate matches one of Google's published root certificates, that each certificate in the chain is signed by the next certificate's key, and that no certificate has been revoked. Google publishes a certificate revocation list (CRL) for compromised attestation keys — if a device's attestation capability is compromised (e.g., key extraction from a TEE vulnerability), Google adds the affected certificates to the revocation list.

**Root certificate transition (2026):** Google announced that a new root certificate using ECDSA P-384 will take effect on 1 February 2026, replacing the existing RSA-4096 and ECDSA P-256 roots. RPs must be prepared to accept certificate chains rooted in either the legacy or the new root during the transition period.

**Remote Key Provisioning (RKP):** Starting with Android 12, Google introduced Remote Key Provisioning — rather than burning long-lived attestation keys into the device at the factory, Google provisions short-lived, per-app ECDSA P-256 attestation certificates over the network via Google Play Services. Android 14+ requires all devices to implement RKP. This architecture limits the blast radius of a key compromise — a compromised device's attestation certificates expire quickly rather than remaining valid for the device's entire lifetime.

##### 13.1.2 Attestation Extension: OID 1.3.6.1.4.1.11129.2.1.17

The key attestation certificate (Certificate 0 in the chain) contains a custom X.509 extension identified by OID `1.3.6.1.4.1.11129.2.1.17`. This extension is DER-encoded and contains a `KeyDescription` ASN.1 structure with the following fields:

```asn1
KeyDescription ::= SEQUENCE {
    attestationVersion         INTEGER,
    attestationSecurityLevel   SecurityLevel,
    keyMintVersion             INTEGER,
    keyMintSecurityLevel       SecurityLevel,
    attestationChallenge       OCTET STRING,
    uniqueId                   OCTET STRING,
    softwareEnforced           AuthorizationList,
    hardwareEnforced           AuthorizationList
}

SecurityLevel ::= ENUMERATED {
    Software       (0),
    TrustedEnvironment (1),
    StrongBox      (2)
}
```

**Field descriptions:**

| Field | Description |
|:------|:-----------|
| `attestationVersion` | Schema version of the attestation data. Version 300+ uses the KeyMint naming convention (replacing Keymaster) |
| `attestationSecurityLevel` | Security level of the environment that produced the attestation — `Software` (no hardware backing), `TrustedEnvironment` (TEE), or `StrongBox` (dedicated SE) |
| `keyMintVersion` | Version of the KeyMint HAL implementation (successor to Keymaster HAL) |
| `keyMintSecurityLevel` | Security level of the KeyMint implementation itself |
| `attestationChallenge` | The server-provided nonce passed during key generation — the RP verifies this matches the challenge it issued to prevent replay attacks |
| `uniqueId` | Optional; a privacy-preserving identifier rotated monthly. Rarely populated in consumer devices |
| `softwareEnforced` | Authorization list of key properties enforced by the Android OS (software layer) |
| `hardwareEnforced` | Authorization list of key properties enforced by TEE/StrongBox hardware. Properties in this list have the strongest guarantee — they cannot be overridden by a compromised OS |

**The `AuthorizationList` structure** contains the key's cryptographic properties and the device state at key generation:

| Authorization Tag | Description | RP Verification Use |
|:-----------------|:-----------|:-------------------|
| `purpose` | Permitted key operations (SIGN, ENCRYPT, DECRYPT, VERIFY) | Confirm the key is restricted to its intended use (e.g., signing only) |
| `algorithm` | Cryptographic algorithm (RSA, EC, AES, HMAC) | Verify the key uses the expected algorithm |
| `keySize` | Key size in bits | Confirm minimum key size requirements |
| `digest` | Permitted hash algorithms (SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512) | Verify acceptable digest algorithms |
| `origin` | Where the key was generated — `GENERATED` (inside hardware), `IMPORTED`, `UNKNOWN` | **Critical:** confirm `origin == GENERATED` to ensure the key was created inside the hardware boundary, not imported from software |
| `osVersion` | Android OS version at key generation time (format: MMmmpp — Major, minor, patch) | Enforce minimum OS version requirements |
| `osPatchLevel` | Android security patch level (format: YYYYMM) | Enforce minimum patch level — reject devices with outdated security patches |
| `attestationApplicationId` | The signing certificate hash and package name of the application that created the key | Verify the key was created by the expected application — prevents a malicious app from claiming keys generated by the legitimate app |
| `rootOfTrust` | Contains the verified boot state: `verifiedBootKey`, `deviceLocked`, `verifiedBootState` (Verified, SelfSigned, Unverified, Failed) | **Critical:** confirm `verifiedBootState == Verified` and `deviceLocked == true` — a device with an unlocked bootloader or modified boot image cannot be trusted for high-assurance attestation |

##### 13.1.3 Security Levels

The `attestationSecurityLevel` and `keyMintSecurityLevel` fields classify the hardware boundary protecting the attestation and key operations:

| Security Level | Hardware Boundary | Protection Guarantee |
|:--------------|:-----------------|:--------------------|
| **Software** (0) | None — attestation produced by Android OS software | ❌ No hardware protection. The attestation is only as trustworthy as the OS. A rooted device can forge software-level attestations |
| **TrustedEnvironment** (1) | ARM TrustZone TEE | ✅ Key operations occur inside the TEE's Secure World. Resistant to OS-level compromise — a rooted device cannot extract TEE-resident keys. Vulnerable to sophisticated TEE exploits (rare but documented — e.g., Qualcomm QSEE vulnerabilities) |
| **StrongBox** (2) | Dedicated Secure Element chip | ✅✅ Strongest protection. SE is physically isolated from the application processor. Resistant to both OS compromise and TEE exploits. Certified to CC EAL5+ in most implementations |

**RP enforcement strategy:** High-assurance RPs (banking, government identity) should require `attestationSecurityLevel >= TrustedEnvironment` and `origin == GENERATED` in the `hardwareEnforced` list. For maximum assurance, require `StrongBox` — though this excludes devices without a dedicated SE chip.

##### 13.1.4 Key Attestation vs. ID Attestation

Android supports two distinct attestation mechanisms:

- **Key attestation** (standard) — attests properties of a cryptographic key: where it was generated, its capabilities, the device's security state at generation time. Available to all applications. The attestation proves the key's security properties but does not identify the specific device

- **ID attestation** (restricted) — attests device-identifying information: serial number, IMEI, MEID, manufacturer, model. ID attestation requires `DEVICE_ID_ATTESTATION` capability and is restricted to **device owner apps** (enterprise MDM) or **zero-touch enrollment** scenarios. Consumer apps cannot request ID attestation

ID attestation fields appear in the `AuthorizationList` as `deviceBrand`, `deviceModel`, `deviceProduct`, `serialNumber`, `imei`, and `meid` — but only when the calling app has the device owner privilege. This restriction prevents consumer apps from using attestation as a device fingerprinting mechanism.

##### 13.1.5 Provisioned Attestation Key Revocation

When a TEE or SE vulnerability is discovered that could allow attestation key extraction, Google revokes the affected attestation keys by adding them to the public attestation certificate revocation status database. Google distributes revocation information through:

1. **Certificate Revocation List (CRL)** — published at a well-known URL; RPs should check the CRL during certificate chain validation
2. **Google Play Services updates** — compromised devices receive updates that disable the affected attestation keys and provision new ones (via Remote Key Provisioning)
3. **FIDO Metadata Service (MDS)** — for WebAuthn attestation (§10.5), Google publishes status reports for affected AAGUIDs

This revocation architecture ensures that a TEE vulnerability discovered in one chipset vendor's implementation (e.g., a Qualcomm QSEE exploit) results in the revocation of attestation certificates for affected devices without impacting devices from other manufacturers.

#### 13.2 Apple App Attest and DeviceCheck

Apple provides two complementary mechanisms for servers to establish trust in client devices: **App Attest** (cryptographic proof of device and app authenticity) and **DeviceCheck** (persistent device-level state tracking). Together, they enable fraud prevention, API abuse protection, and device trust decisions — though they differ fundamentally in their security model and capabilities.

##### 13.2.1 App Attest (iOS 14+)

App Attest allows an app to generate a cryptographic key pair inside the Secure Enclave and have Apple certify that the key was generated on a genuine Apple device running an unmodified copy of the app. The attestation is bound to the specific app identity (`teamID.bundleID`), creating a cryptographic link between the device, the app binary, and the key.

**Key generation:**

```swift
let attestService = DCAppAttestService.shared
guard attestService.isSupported else { return }

attestService.generateKey { keyId, error in
    // keyId: a unique identifier for the P-256 key pair
    // generated inside the Secure Enclave
    // The private key never leaves the SE
}
```

The `generateKey()` method creates a P-256 (secp256r1) ECDSA key pair inside the Secure Enclave. The `keyId` returned is a reference handle — the private key material is non-exportable and never accessible to the application or the operating system.

**Attestation ceremony:**

```swift
// Server provides a one-time challenge (nonce)
let clientDataHash = Data(SHA256.hash(data: challengeFromServer))

attestService.attestKey(keyId, clientDataHash: clientDataHash) { attestationObject, error in
    // attestationObject: CBOR-encoded attestation (WebAuthn format)
    // Send to server for verification
}
```

The `attestKey(_:clientDataHash:)` method sends the key's public key and the client data hash to Apple's attestation service. Apple verifies the device's authenticity, generates an attestation object, and returns it to the app. The attestation object is CBOR-encoded following the WebAuthn attestation object format.

**Attestation object structure:**

```
Attestation Object (CBOR map):
├── fmt: "apple-appattest"
├── attStmt:
│   ├── x5c: [attestation certificate, intermediate CA certificate]
│   │   └── Certificate chain rooted in Apple App Attestation Root CA
│   └── receipt: opaque receipt for risk metric queries
└── authData:
    ├── rpIdHash: SHA-256(teamID.bundleID)  ← binds to app identity
    ├── flags: UP (user present), AT (attested credential data present)
    ├── counter: 0  ← initial value; increments with each assertion
    ├── aaguid: "appattest\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00" (production)
    │           or "appattestdevelop" (development)
    └── credentialId: SHA-256(public key)
```

**Server-side verification steps:**

1. Decode the CBOR attestation object
2. Extract the certificate chain from `attStmt.x5c`
3. Verify the certificate chain roots in Apple's App Attestation Root CA
4. Compute `nonce = SHA-256(authData || clientDataHash)` and verify it matches the nonce in the attestation certificate's OID `1.2.840.113635.100.8.2` extension
5. Verify `rpIdHash == SHA-256(teamID + "." + bundleID)` — confirms the attestation is bound to the correct app
6. Verify `counter == 0` for initial attestation
7. Check `aaguid` to distinguish production from development environment
8. Store the `credentialId` (public key hash) and `counter` for subsequent assertion verification

##### 13.2.2 App Attest Assertions

After the initial attestation, the app uses the attested key to **sign assertions** — cryptographic proofs that specific requests originated from the attested device/app pair:

```swift
let clientDataHash = Data(SHA256.hash(data: requestPayload))

attestService.generateAssertion(keyId, clientDataHash: clientDataHash) { assertion, error in
    // assertion: CBOR-encoded assertion object
    // Include in API request to server
}
```

The assertion contains the `authenticatorData` (with an incremented counter) and a signature over the concatenation of `authenticatorData` and `clientDataHash`, signed by the Secure Enclave-resident private key. The server verifies the signature using the stored public key, checks that the counter has incremented monotonically (counter reuse indicates a cloned assertion), and validates the `rpIdHash`.

**Use cases:**

- **API abuse prevention** — only genuine app instances can call protected server endpoints; automated scripts and modified app binaries cannot produce valid assertions
- **In-app purchase validation** — the server verifies that purchase requests originate from a legitimate app, not a jailbroken device or modified binary
- **Promotional offer protection** — one-time offers (e.g., free trial, referral bonus) are bound to attested device/app pairs, preventing abuse through device cloning or app modification
- **Game integrity** — leaderboard submissions and competitive game actions are signed by attested keys, preventing score manipulation

##### 13.2.3 DeviceCheck (iOS 11+)

DeviceCheck is a simpler, non-cryptographic mechanism that allows a server to associate **two bits of persistent state** with each device, per developer. Unlike App Attest, DeviceCheck does not provide cryptographic proof of device authenticity — it provides a device uniqueness signal.

**Client-side token generation:**

```swift
DCDevice.current.generateToken { token, error in
    // token: device-specific, short-lived token
    // Send to server for use with Apple's DeviceCheck API
}
```

**Server-side API interaction:**

The server sends the device token to Apple's DeviceCheck API (`https://api.devicecheck.apple.com/v1/`) along with its developer credentials. Apple maintains a mapping from device to two boolean bits per developer. The server can:

- **Query bits** — retrieve the current values of `bit0` and `bit1` for this device
- **Update bits** — set `bit0` and/or `bit1` to true or false

**Use cases:**

| Bit Configuration | Meaning (Example) |
|:-----------------|:-----------------|
| `bit0 = false, bit1 = false` | New device — never seen before |
| `bit0 = true, bit1 = false` | Device has redeemed a promotional offer |
| `bit0 = true, bit1 = true` | Device has been flagged for fraudulent activity |

DeviceCheck bits persist across app reinstallation and device reset (they are tied to the hardware, not the app installation). This makes DeviceCheck effective for preventing account fraud — a device flagged as fraudulent remains flagged even after a factory reset and fresh app install.

##### 13.2.4 App Attest vs. DeviceCheck Comparison

| Dimension | App Attest | DeviceCheck |
|:----------|:----------|:-----------|
| **Availability** | iOS 14+ | iOS 11+ |
| **Security model** | Cryptographic attestation — Secure Enclave key pair, certificate chain rooted in Apple CA | Token-based uniqueness signal — no cryptographic identity |
| **What it proves** | Device is genuine Apple hardware running an unmodified copy of the specific app | Device is a real Apple device (weaker claim — no app identity binding) |
| **Persistent state** | None — App Attest provides identity, not state | Two bits per device per developer, persisted by Apple |
| **Replay protection** | ✅ Monotonic counter in assertions; nonce in attestation | ⚠️ Token is short-lived but does not provide per-request binding |
| **Anti-jailbreak** | ✅ Attestation fails on jailbroken devices (Apple's attestation service rejects them) | ❌ Jailbroken devices can generate DeviceCheck tokens |
| **Primary use** | API abuse prevention, request signing, app integrity verification | Device fraud tracking, one-time offer enforcement |

#### 13.3 TPM 2.0 Attestation

The Trusted Platform Module (TPM) 2.0 — introduced architecturally in §11.2.3 — provides two forms of attestation: **platform attestation** (proving the device's boot state and software integrity) and **key attestation** (proving that a specific cryptographic key was generated inside the TPM). These mechanisms are standardised by the Trusted Computing Group (TCG) in ISO/IEC 11889 and are the foundation of enterprise remote attestation, measured boot, and conditional access.

##### 13.3.1 Platform Attestation: Remote Attestation via TPM2_Quote

Platform attestation — also called **remote attestation** — allows a verifier to obtain cryptographic proof of the device's current software state. The TPM produces a signed statement of its Platform Configuration Register (PCR) values, which encode the measurements of every component loaded during the device's boot process (§11.2.3 for PCR definitions).

**Quote generation:**

```
TPM2_Quote(
    signHandle   = AK_handle,          // Attestation Key (AK) — signs the quote
    qualifyingData = server_nonce,      // Server-provided nonce — prevents replay
    PCRselect    = {PCR[0], PCR[1], PCR[2], PCR[4], PCR[7], PCR[11]}
)
```

The `TPM2_Quote` command instructs the TPM to:
1. Read the current values of the selected PCRs
2. Compute a SHA-256 digest over the concatenation of the selected PCR values
3. Construct a `TPMS_ATTEST` structure containing the PCR digest, the qualifying data (nonce), and metadata about the signing key
4. Sign the `TPMS_ATTEST` structure with the Attestation Key (AK)

**`TPMS_ATTEST` structure:**

```
TPMS_ATTEST {
    magic          = 0xFF544347,        // "TCG" magic value — identifies this as a TPM-generated attestation
    type           = TPM_ST_ATTEST_QUOTE,
    qualifiedSigner = AK_qualified_name, // Identifies the AK that signed this quote
    extraData      = server_nonce,       // The server-provided nonce — RP verifies this matches
    clockInfo      = {
        clock      = tpm_clock_value,    // TPM's internal clock (milliseconds since last TPM2_Clear)
        resetCount = n,                  // Number of TPM resets
        restartCount = m                 // Number of TPM restarts
    },
    firmwareVersion = tpm_fw_version,
    attested       = TPMS_QUOTE_INFO {
        pcrSelect  = selected_PCRs,      // Which PCRs were quoted
        pcrDigest  = SHA-256(PCR_values)  // Digest of the quoted PCR values
    }
}
```

**Server-side verification workflow:**

1. **Validate the AK certificate chain** — the AK's certificate must chain to the TPM manufacturer's root CA (Infineon, STMicroelectronics, NXP, Intel, AMD). The Endorsement Key (EK) certificate, provisioned by the manufacturer, vouches for the AK's association with a genuine TPM
2. **Verify the quote signature** — validate the `TPMS_ATTEST` signature using the AK's public key. A valid signature proves the quote was generated inside the TPM
3. **Check the nonce** — verify `extraData == server_nonce`. Nonce mismatch indicates replay
4. **Verify PCR values** — compare the `pcrDigest` against **expected ("golden") PCR measurements**. The verifier maintains a database of known-good PCR configurations for approved firmware versions, bootloader versions, and OS builds. If the PCR values match, the device's boot chain is unmodified
5. **Validate against the event log** — the device provides a **TCG Event Log** alongside the quote — a sequential record of every measurement extended into each PCR. The verifier replays the event log (re-computing `PCR_new = SHA-256(PCR_old || measurement)` for each entry) to confirm the event log produces the quoted PCR values. This replay ensures the event log is consistent with the PCRs and allows the verifier to identify exactly which software components were loaded

##### 13.3.2 Key Attestation: TPM2_Certify

TPM key attestation proves that a specific key was generated inside the TPM and has particular properties (non-exportable, hardware-generated, bound to a specific parent key).

```
TPM2_Certify(
    objectHandle = target_key_handle,   // The key being attested
    signHandle   = AK_handle            // The AK that signs the certify statement
)
```

The TPM produces a `TPMS_ATTEST` structure with `type = TPM_ST_ATTEST_CERTIFY` containing:

- **`objectName`** — the cryptographic name of the attested key (a hash of its public area)
- **`qualifiedName`** — the full qualified name including the key's position in the TPM's key hierarchy
- **Key properties** — derived from the key's `TPMA_OBJECT` attributes:
  - `fixedTPM` — the key cannot be duplicated to another TPM
  - `fixedParent` — the key cannot be moved to a different parent key
  - `sensitiveDataOrigin` — the key's sensitive data (private key material) was generated by the TPM, not imported
  - `userWithAuth` — the key requires user authorisation (PIN, password, or HMAC session) for each use

**RP verification:** After validating the AK signature, the RP checks that the attested key's properties include `fixedTPM`, `fixedParent`, and `sensitiveDataOrigin` — confirming the key is truly hardware-bound and was generated inside the TPM (not imported from software).

##### 13.3.3 Endorsement Key (EK) Certificate Chain

Every TPM contains a unique **Endorsement Key (EK)** — an asymmetric key pair provisioned by the TPM manufacturer during fabrication. The EK certificate is signed by the manufacturer's CA, creating a certificate chain that establishes the TPM's authenticity:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EK["`**EK Certificate**
    **Subject**: TPM EK public key (unique per TPM)
    **Issuer**: TPM Manufacturer Intermediate CA
    **Signed by**: manufacturer intermediate key`"]
    style EK text-align:left

    INT["`**Manufacturer Intermediate CA**
    **Issuer**: Manufacturer Root CA
    **Signed by**: manufacturer root key`"]
    style INT text-align:left

    ROOT["`**Manufacturer Root CA**
    (e.g., Infineon, NXP, STMicro, Intel, AMD)
    Self-signed root certificate`"]
    style ROOT text-align:left

    EK -->|Signed By| INT
    INT -->|Signed By| ROOT
```

The EK is used exclusively for **encryption and key agreement** — it cannot sign data directly. To produce attestation signatures, the TPM derives **Attestation Keys (AKs)** from the EK. The AK certificate chain extends the EK chain by one level, with the AK certified as belonging to the same TPM as the EK.

**TPM manufacturer CAs:** Each major TPM chip vendor operates its own certificate hierarchy:

| Vendor | TPM Products | Root CA |
|:-------|:------------|:--------|
| Infineon Technologies | OPTIGA TPM (SLB 9670, SLB 9672) | Infineon OPTIGA RSA Root CA / ECC Root CA |
| STMicroelectronics | ST33 series TPM | STMicroelectronics TPM Root CA |
| NXP Semiconductors | EdgeLock SE050 (TPM-compatible) | NXP Semiconductors TPM Root CA |
| Intel | Platform Trust Technology (PTT) — firmware TPM integrated in Intel ME/CSME | Intel TPM Root CA |
| AMD | fTPM (firmware TPM in AMD Secure Processor / PSP) | AMD TPM Root CA |
| Microsoft | Pluton Security Processor (TPM-compatible, integrated in select AMD/Intel/Qualcomm CPUs) | Microsoft Pluton Root CA |

##### 13.3.4 Privacy-Preserving Attestation

Standard TPM attestation using the EK or a directly certified AK reveals a persistent device identity to the verifier — the same EK certificate appears in every attestation, enabling cross-service tracking. The TCG specification provides two mechanisms to mitigate this privacy concern:

**Direct Anonymous Attestation (DAA):**

DAA is a zero-knowledge cryptographic protocol that allows a TPM to prove it is a genuine TPM — i.e., it possesses a valid credential issued by a trusted authority — without revealing which specific TPM it is. The protocol uses group signatures based on elliptic curve cryptography (ECC-DAA in TPM 2.0):

1. **Issuer (Privacy CA)** — a trusted third party that verifies the TPM's EK certificate and issues a DAA credential. The credential certifies that the holder is a genuine TPM without embedding any identifying information
2. **Prover (TPM)** — generates a DAA signature on the attestation data using its DAA credential. The signature proves membership in the "genuine TPM" group without revealing the individual identity
